image_placeholder: A rustic casserole dish of mushroom bourguignon with golden mushrooms, pearl onions, and carrots in a rich, dark wine sauce, with crusty bread alongside.
wine_pairing_notes: A medium-bodied Pinot Noir from Burgundy is the natural pairing.
tips: Sear the mushrooms properly — they should be deeply golden, not steamed.
A good red wine makes all the difference — use something you'd drink.
Like all braises, this tastes even better the next day.

title: Jackfruit Tacos
description: Young green jackfruit has a remarkable ability to mimic pulled meat when braised in smoky, spicy sauces. These vegan tacos use jackfruit cooked in a chipotle-adobo sauce until tender and shredded, then served in corn tortillas with pickled red onion, avocado, and coriander. They deliver all the satisfaction of carnitas tacos without any animal products.

ingredients.name: tinned young green jackfruit
ingredients.notes: in water or brine, drained and rinsed

steps.instruction: Drain and rinse jackfruit. Squeeze out excess water and shred with a fork into pulled-meat-like pieces.
Heat oil in a pan. Fry garlic for 30 seconds, add cumin and smoked paprika. Cook 30 seconds. Add jackfruit and toss to coat. Add chopped chipotles, adobo sauce, and tinned tomatoes.
Simmer for 20 minutes, stirring and pressing with a spatula to break down the jackfruit further. The sauce should reduce and the jackfruit should look like shredded meat. Add lime juice and salt.
Warm tortillas. Fill with pulled jackfruit, sliced avocado, pickled red onion, and coriander. Serve with lime wedges.

image_placeholder: Corn tortillas filled with smoky pulled jackfruit, topped with sliced avocado, pink pickled onion, and fresh coriander.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Mexican lager or a lime agua fresca.
tips: Use young green jackfruit in water/brine, NOT ripe jackfruit (which is sweet and fruity).
Press and shred the jackfruit thoroughly for the most meat-like texture.
Chipotle chillies in adobo are essential for the smoky flavour — available in tins at most supermarkets.

title: Tom Kha Jay
description: This plant-based version of the classic tom kha gai replaces chicken with tofu and mushrooms, while maintaining the essential aromatic broth of coconut milk, galangal, lemongrass, and kaffir lime leaves. It is just as fragrant, rich, and satisfying as the original — a perfect demonstration of how Thai soups can be naturally adapted for vegan diets without losing any of their complexity.

steps.instruction: Combine stock and coconut milk in a pot. Add galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, and chillies. Bring to a gentle simmer and infuse for 5 minutes.
Add tofu, mushrooms, and cherry tomatoes. Simmer gently for 8 minutes.
Remove from heat. Add soy sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar. Garnish with coriander and serve.

image_placeholder: A bowl of creamy vegan tom kha soup with tofu, mushrooms, and cherry tomatoes in a pale coconut broth, garnished with coriander and lemongrass.
wine_pairing_notes: An off-dry Riesling or a cold Thai beer.
tips: Soy sauce replaces fish sauce for a fully vegan version with similar salty depth.
Don't boil vigorously — gentle simmering keeps the coconut milk smooth.
This soup is naturally gluten-free if you use tamari instead of soy sauce.

title: Misir Wot
description: Misir wot is Ethiopia's beloved vegan lentil stew — red lentils simmered in a rich berbere and onion sauce until thick and deeply flavoured. It is a staple during Ethiopian fasting periods (when animal products are forbidden) and is served on injera as part of a communal platter. Despite being entirely plant-based, it is remarkably rich and satisfying thanks to the slowly caramelised onions and aromatic spice blend.

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp paprika + 1 tsp cayenne + 0.5 tsp cumin + 0.5 tsp fenugreek

steps.instruction: In a dry, heavy pot, cook diced onions over medium heat for 10-12 minutes, stirring frequently, until dry, soft, and starting to brown. Do not add oil yet.
Add oil, berbere, garlic, ginger, and tomato purée. Cook for 3 minutes, stirring constantly.

Add lentils and water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer. Cook for 25-30 minutes, stirring frequently (lentils stick easily), until the lentils are completely broken down and the stew is thick. Add water if needed.
Season with salt. Serve on injera or with rice.

image_placeholder: A bowl of rich, dark red misir wot (lentil stew) served on a piece of injera, as part of an Ethiopian platter with colourful vegetable sides.
wine_pairing_notes: Ethiopian honey wine (tej), or a glass of cold hibiscus drink.
tips: Dry-cooking the onions first (without oil) is the authentic Ethiopian technique that builds deep flavour.
Stir frequently in the last 10 minutes — red lentils stick easily to the bottom.
This freezes beautifully and is perfect for meal prep.

title: Mediterranean Stuffed Peppers
description: These vegan stuffed peppers are filled with a fragrant mixture of rice, sun-dried tomatoes, olives, capers, pine nuts, and fresh herbs, baked until the peppers are tender and the filling is golden. Drawing on flavours from across the Mediterranean, they are vibrant, nutritious, and entirely plant-based. Perfect as a main course or an impressive side dish.

ingredients.name: large bell peppers
ingredients.notes: mixed colours, tops cut off and deseeded

steps.instruction: Preheat oven to 190°C. Combine cooked rice, sun-dried tomatoes, olives, capers, pine nuts, garlic, basil, parsley, 2 tbsp olive oil, lemon juice, and smoked paprika. Season with salt and pepper.
Stuff each pepper with the rice mixture, pressing down gently. Place in a baking dish. Drizzle with remaining olive oil and pour 100 ml water into the base of the dish.
Cover with foil and bake for 30 minutes. Remove foil and bake for a further 15 minutes until the peppers are tender and the tops are slightly golden.
Serve warm or at room temperature, drizzled with extra olive oil.

image_placeholder: Colourful stuffed bell peppers in a baking dish, golden on top, showing the rice, olive, and sun-dried tomato filling, drizzled with olive oil.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Greek Assyrtiko or an Italian Vermentino.
tips: Use a mix of red, yellow, and orange peppers for the most attractive presentation.
The water in the baking dish creates steam that helps cook the peppers evenly.
These are excellent at room temperature — great for picnics and packed lunches.

title: Bún Chả
description: Bún chả is Hanoi's iconic noodle dish — grilled pork patties and sliced pork belly served over cold rice vermicelli with a light, tangy dipping broth, fresh herbs, and pickled vegetables. This naturally gluten-free bowl is a masterclass in Vietnamese balance — smoky grilled meat, cool herbs, crunchy vegetables, and a sweet-sour-savoury broth, all coming together in every bite.

steps.instruction: Marinate pork belly slices in 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, garlic, and black pepper for 30 minutes. Shape minced pork into small patties and marinate similarly.

Make the dipping broth: dissolve 2 tbsp sugar in warm water. Add 3 tbsp fish sauce, lime juice, rice vinegar, sliced chilli, and minced garlic. Quick-pickle the julienned carrots and daikon in a little vinegar and sugar.
Grill pork belly slices and patties over high heat (or under a grill) for 3-4 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Divide cold rice noodles between bowls. Top with grilled pork, pickled vegetables, fresh herbs, and lettuce. Serve with the dipping broth on the side or poured over.

image_placeholder: A vibrant Vietnamese noodle bowl with charred pork patties, cool rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, and pickled vegetables, with a small bowl of dipping broth on the side.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Saigon beer or a crisp dry Riesling.
tips: The dipping broth should be balanced — sweet, sour, salty, and spicy in equal measure.
Serve noodles cold or at room temperature — the contrast with hot, charred pork is key.
This is a naturally gluten-free dish — just ensure the fish sauce is gluten-free (most are).

title: Peruvian Quinoa Salad
title_en: Peruvian Quinoa Salad with Ají Amarillo
description: This vibrant gluten-free salad celebrates quinoa in its homeland of Peru. Fluffy quinoa is tossed with black beans, corn, avocado, red onion, and a zesty ají amarillo-lime dressing. It is nutritious, colourful, and packed with protein from the quinoa and beans. Perfect as a light main or a substantial side dish, it showcases how Peruvian flavours naturally lend themselves to healthy, gluten-free eating.

steps.instruction: Cook quinoa in 400 ml salted water for 15 minutes until tender and the water is absorbed. Fluff with a fork and cool to room temperature.
Whisk together lime juice, olive oil, ají amarillo paste, and salt for the dressing.
Combine cooled quinoa with black beans, corn, red onion, cherry tomatoes, and coriander. Pour dressing over and toss gently. Fold in avocado last.
Serve at room temperature or chilled. Adjust seasoning with extra lime and salt.

image_placeholder: A colourful quinoa salad in a bowl with black beans, corn, diced avocado, cherry tomatoes, and coriander, drizzled with a golden lime dressing.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Sauvignon Blanc or a cold lime agua fresca.
tips: Rinse quinoa thoroughly before cooking to remove its natural bitter coating.
Add avocado just before serving to prevent browning.
This salad is ideal for meal prep — make the base and add avocado daily.

title: Tamago Gohan
description: Tamago gohan (egg rice) is Japan's ultimate comfort food — a bowl of steaming hot rice with a raw egg stirred in, seasoned with soy sauce. The heat of the rice gently cooks the egg into a silky, golden coating around each grain. This humble dish appears in Japanese homes as a quick breakfast or late-night snack, and is naturally gluten-free when made with tamari. It is simplicity perfected.

steps.instruction: Divide piping hot rice between two bowls.
Make a small well in the centre of each bowl. Crack a raw egg into each well. Add tamari.
Stir vigorously with chopsticks for 30 seconds until the egg is thoroughly combined and the rice is coated in a silky, golden layer. The heat of the rice partially cooks the egg.
Top with spring onion, sesame seeds, and furikake. Eat immediately while still warm.

image_placeholder: A Japanese bowl of piping hot white rice with a golden raw egg stirred in, topped with sliced spring onion and sesame seeds.
wine_pairing_notes: A cup of hot green tea or miso soup on the side.
tips: The rice MUST be freshly cooked and piping hot — this is what partially cooks the egg.
Use the freshest possible eggs — sashimi-grade freshness is ideal.
Japanese short-grain rice is essential — its sticky texture and sweetness are integral.

title: Chana Masala
description: Chana masala is one of India's most popular vegetarian dishes — chickpeas simmered in a tangy, spiced tomato sauce with cumin, coriander, amchur, and garam masala. It is naturally gluten-free and packed with protein. This Punjabi staple is served with rice, naan, or puri across India and internationally, and is a cornerstone of both home cooking and restaurant menus.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a pan. Fry onions for 8 minutes until golden. Add garlic, ginger, and green chilli. Cook 1 minute.
Add cumin, coriander, chilli powder, and turmeric. Stir for 30 seconds. Add tinned tomatoes and cook for 8-10 minutes until the oil separates from the masala.
Add chickpeas and 200 ml water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes. Mash a few chickpeas against the side of the pan for a thicker gravy.
Add garam masala, amchur, and salt. Cook 2 more minutes. Garnish with fresh coriander and serve with rice.

image_placeholder: A bowl of chana masala — chickpeas in a rich, orange-red tomato sauce, garnished with fresh coriander, served alongside steamed basmati rice.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold lager or a mango lassi.
tips: Mashing a few chickpeas against the pan creates a thicker, more luscious gravy.
Amchur (dried mango powder) gives the characteristic tartness — lemon juice at the end is a good substitute.
This keeps and freezes well — perfect for weekly meal prep.

title: Thai Papaya Salad (Gluten-Free)
description: A naturally gluten-free version of Thailand's beloved som tam — shredded green papaya with a dressing adapted using tamari instead of regular soy sauce. This refreshing salad is bright, crunchy, and explosively flavoured, making it perfect as a light meal or side dish. The pounding technique releases the papaya's juices and allows the dressing to penetrate.

steps.instruction: Pound garlic and chillies in a mortar until roughly broken. Add green beans and bruise lightly.
Add palm sugar, tamari, fish sauce, and lime juice. Pound to dissolve sugar.
Add papaya a handful at a time, pounding and tossing to bruise and coat. Add tomatoes and peanuts. Toss gently.
Taste and adjust sweet-sour-salty-spicy balance. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A mound of shredded green papaya salad with cherry tomatoes and peanuts on a plate, with a lime wedge.
wine_pairing_notes: A cold Thai beer or sparkling water with lime.
tips: Use tamari instead of regular soy sauce to keep this gluten-free.
Mortar and pestle is essential — don't use a food processor.
Serve immediately for maximum crunch.

title: Smashed Avocado Toast with Dukkah
description: Australia's gift to modern brunch culture — thick sourdough toast topped with creamy smashed avocado, a generous sprinkle of Egyptian dukkah (a nut and spice blend), chilli flakes, and a squeeze of lemon. Simple, nutritious, and endlessly customisable, it has become a global phenomenon. The combination of creamy avocado with crunchy, aromatic dukkah elevates this from simple toast to something genuinely special.

steps.instruction: Toast sourdough until golden and crispy.
Halve avocados, remove stones. Scoop flesh onto the toast and smash roughly with a fork — keep it chunky.
Squeeze lemon juice over, drizzle with olive oil. Sprinkle generously with dukkah, chilli flakes, and flaky salt. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: Thick sourdough toast topped with chunky smashed avocado, sprinkled with golden dukkah and chilli flakes, drizzled with olive oil, on a rustic wooden board.
wine_pairing_notes: A flat white coffee is the classic pairing.
tips: Keep the avocado chunky — overworking it makes it paste-like.
Dukkah is available in most supermarkets or easily made at home by toasting and grinding hazelnuts, sesame, cumin, and coriander.
Add a poached egg on top for a more substantial meal.

title: Misoshiru
description: Miso soup is the foundation of Japanese cuisine — a simple, nourishing broth of dashi (kombu and bonito stock) enriched with miso paste, tofu, and wakame seaweed. It accompanies virtually every Japanese meal and takes less than 10 minutes to make. Despite its simplicity, the depth of umami from the dashi and the complexity of the miso create a soup of remarkable sophistication.

ingredients.name: dashi stock
ingredients.notes: from kombu and bonito flakes, or instant dashi

steps.instruction: Bring dashi stock to a gentle simmer (do not boil vigorously). Add tofu cubes and drained wakame. Heat through for 2 minutes.
Remove from heat. Dissolve miso paste in a ladle of hot stock, then stir back into the pot. Never boil miso — it kills the beneficial bacteria and dulls the flavour.
Ladle into bowls and top with sliced spring onion. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A Japanese lacquer bowl of clear miso soup with visible tofu cubes, wakame seaweed, and spring onion slices floating in the golden broth.
wine_pairing_notes: A cup of green tea is the traditional accompaniment.
tips: Never boil miso — add it off the heat and stir to dissolve.
Good dashi is the foundation. If using instant, choose a reputable Japanese brand.
Miso soup is meant to be drunk from the bowl in Japan, with chopsticks for the solid bits.

title: Dakos
description: Dakos is Crete's beloved summer salad — dried barley rusks (paximadi) soaked with ripe tomatoes, topped with crumbled feta, olives, capers, and oregano, then drizzled with olive oil. It is the Greek islands' answer to Italian bruschetta, and a perfect example of how simple, quality ingredients create extraordinary food. It takes minutes to prepare and is the taste of a Cretan summer.

ingredients.name: barley rusks (paximadi)
ingredients.notes: large, or use thick wholemeal toast

ingredients.name: ripe tomatoes
ingredients.notes: large, grated on a box grater (discard skin)

steps.instruction: Briefly run the barley rusks under water for 5 seconds (or sprinkle with water) — they should be slightly softened but still crunchy inside. Place on plates.
Spoon grated tomato over each rusk. Top with crumbled feta, olives, and capers.
Drizzle generously with olive oil. Sprinkle with oregano, salt, and pepper. Serve immediately.

image_placeholder: A barley rusk topped with grated tomato, crumbled white feta, dark olives, and capers, drizzled with olive oil and sprinkled with oregano, on a blue plate.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Greek Assyrtiko from Santorini.
tips: Use the ripest, most flavourful tomatoes you can find — they are the star of this dish.
Grating the tomatoes on a box grater is the traditional Cretan technique — it creates a juicy pulp.
Don't over-soak the rusks — they should be slightly softened but still have crunch.

title: Gỏi Cuốn
description: Vietnamese summer rolls are fresh, unfried rice paper rolls filled with prawns, vermicelli noodles, herbs, and crunchy vegetables, served with a peanut dipping sauce. They are light, refreshing, and naturally gluten-free — the perfect appetiser or light meal. The translucent rice paper reveals the colourful filling, making them as beautiful as they are delicious.

steps.instruction: Make the dipping sauce: whisk peanut butter, hoisin sauce, warm water, lime juice, and chilli flakes until smooth.
Dip one rice paper wrapper in a bowl of warm water for 5-10 seconds until just pliable (not too soft). Place flat on a clean surface.
Place a lettuce leaf, a small bundle of vermicelli, carrot, cucumber, herbs, and 4 prawn halves (pink side down) in the centre lower third of the wrapper.
Fold the bottom edge over the filling, fold in the sides, then roll tightly upwards. The wrapper seals to itself. Repeat with remaining wrappers.
Serve immediately with the peanut dipping sauce. Cut in half on the diagonal for presentation.

image_placeholder: Translucent Vietnamese summer rolls cut in half showing pink prawns, green herbs, and white vermicelli inside, arranged on a plate with a bowl of peanut dipping sauce.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Grüner Veltliner or a cold Vietnamese beer.
tips: Don't over-soak the rice paper — it should be pliable but still slightly firm. It continues to soften.
Work on a damp tea towel to prevent sticking.
Summer rolls don't keep — the wrappers dry out and become tough. Make and eat within 30 minutes.

title: Bruschetta Trio
description: A trio of classic Italian bruschetta — grilled bread rubbed with garlic and topped with three different seasonal toppings: classic tomato-basil, creamy ricotta with honey and walnuts, and roasted red pepper with olives. Each represents a different flavour profile, making this the perfect quick appetiser or light meal. The key is quality bread, perfectly grilled, and the freshest toppings.

ingredients.name: walnuts
ingredients.notes: toasted, roughly chopped, for ricotta topping

ingredients.name: Kalamata olives
ingredients.notes: pitted and chopped, for pepper topping

steps.instruction: Grill or toast bread slices until golden and slightly charred on both sides. Immediately rub one side with cut garlic and drizzle with olive oil.
For the tomato bruschetta: toss diced tomatoes with basil, a drizzle of olive oil, and salt. Spoon onto 4 slices.
For the ricotta bruschetta: spread ricotta on 4 slices, drizzle with honey, top with toasted walnuts and a crack of black pepper.
For the pepper bruschetta: top 4 slices with roasted red peppers and chopped olives. Drizzle with balsamic glaze if using. Serve all three varieties on a platter.

image_placeholder: A rustic wooden board with three types of bruschetta — red tomato-basil, white ricotta-honey-walnut, and orange roasted pepper-olive — arranged in rows on grilled bread.
wine_pairing_notes: A crisp Vermentino or a chilled Prosecco.
tips: Use the best tomatoes you can find — flavourless tomatoes make flavourless bruschetta.
Rub the garlic while the bread is still hot — the rough surface acts as a grater.
Assemble just before serving to keep the bread crispy.
